---
title: "The Beach BIA: Modeling Human Activity and Community Usage"
subtitle: "Explanatory Modeling, Clustering, and Bayesian Inference"
author: "Derek Kusmenko and Arusan Surendiran"
date: today
format: 
  revealjs:
    theme: default # Equivalent clean look, can also try 'simple' or 'serif'
    slide-number: true
    logo: derek-slides/beach-uoft.png
    css: styles.css # Optional: if you want to add custom CSS later
---

## Project Objectives

* **Economic Vitality:** Understand foot traffic in the Beaches to increase activity for local businesses.
* **Data:** Leveraging Toronto Open Data to justify BIA budgetary decisions.

::: {.callout-note title="Specific Research Questions" icon=false}
1. Can we segment the people coming to the Beaches based off their habits and where they come from?
2. How long do pedestrians stay at the Beaches for?
3. How do external variables (weather, holidays) probabilistically impact pedestrian flow?
4. How these patterns may affect commercial vitality along the strip
:::

## Data

**Case for Open-Source**
<br>
<small>The Beach BIA deliberately avoided commercial mobile phone data, relying exclusively on municipal datasets for The Beach BIA.</small>

* **Privacy & Trust:** Eliminates re-identification risks, vulnerable demographics (youth/seniors) inherently under-represented by mobile tracking.
* To avoid expensive purchasing of data, municipal datasets are purpose-built for urban planning and already tax-funded.

<br>

**Datasets**
<br>
<small>We aggregated multiple open-source domains from **2021--2025** into a single analytical dataset.</small>

* Extracted Open Data Toronto Bike Share records, strictly filtering for trips originating or terminating at Beach BIA docks.
* Merged the core bike data with Historical Climate Data (Environment Canada), Toronto Beach Water Quality metrics.

# Clustering Analysis

## Identifying Patterns: Unsupervised Learning

* **Method:** K-Means clustering was used to create "Trip Profiles" by clustering the data based on trip duration and the hour of the day the trip started. Used elbow method to determine optimal number of clusters at 3.

![K-Means Clusters for Trip Profiles.](kmeans.png){width=60% fig-align="center"}

# Cluster Attributes

## Cluster Attributes

| Cluster | Total Trips | Avg Start Hour | Avg Duration (Mins) | Pct Annual | Pct Casual |
| :---: | :---: | :---: | :---: | :---: | :---: |
| 1 | 40243 | 15.5 | 61.8 | 9.9 | 90.1 |
| 2 | 98882 | 10.7 | 20.9 | 34.0 | 66.0 |
| 3 | 150026 | 17.9 | 21.5 | 30.5 | 69.5 |

: Cluster Profiles {tbl-colwidths="[15, 17, 17, 17, 17, 17]"}

# Dwell Times

## Dwell Times

* Want to quantify dwell time in the Beaches.
* **Issue:** There is no way to track individuals, only individual bike rides. 

![Arrival and Departure Time Distributions.](dwell1.png){width=60% fig-align="center"}

## Dwell Time Analysis

| Direction | Average Hour of Day | Median Hour of Day | Total Trips |
| :--- | :---: | :---: | :---: |
| Inbound | 13.99420 | 14 | 58575 |
| Outbound | 15.41696 | 16 | 45937 |

: Flow Distribution Statistics

* Can approximate dwell time by taking difference in average arrival vs departure.
* Average Dwell time is 1.42 hours, approximately 85 minutes.
* Important to note that 21.6% of riders do not bike back, likely leading to longer true dwell time.

## Cluster Analysis via Dwell Times

:::: {.columns}

::: {.column width="50%"}
![All Days.](dwell2.png)
:::

::: {.column width="50%"}
![Leisure Cluster All Days.](dwell3.png)
:::

::::

## Heatmaps

* Want to understand where people are coming from.
* Most trips are coming in from the East (towards downtown).
* Most common destination is Northern Dancer Blvd/Lake Shore, by Woodbine Beach and Park.

![Top 20 Inbound Leisure Trips.](heatmaps.png){width=60% fig-align="center"}

## Bayesian Model Prediction Accuracy

:::: {.columns}

::: {.column width="50%"}
![Predictive Accuracy of Bayesian Model](preds.png){width=90%}
:::

::: {.column width="50%"}
* Model trained on 2021-2024 data, predicting on 2025 data.
* Able to generate accurate predictions for most days. 
* See more inaccuracy on higher counts, likely that these days are holidays and see much different traffic than usual.
:::

::::

## Exploratory Data Analysis: Climate & Bike Share

![ ](cycle_temp.png){width=55% fig-align="center"}

* Using Generalized Additive Models (GAM), we observe a relationship between Bike Share utilization and temperature throughout 2025. 
* This confirmation supports Dr. Jo’s observations that the Beach BIA struggles to attract visitors during the winter, a challenge prevalent across Toronto.

## GAMs: Addressing Autocorrelation

**Lack of Independence**
* Traditional GLMs assume all observations are strictly independent. For daily ridership, treating sequential days as independent ignores inherent time-series momentum, leading to severe residual autocorrelation.
* We correct this by embedding a 1-day lagged response ($\text{Trips}_{t-1}$) directly into the Negative Binomial structure, explicitly modeling the temporal dependence:

$$
\log(\mu_t) = \text{Environmental \& Calendar Predictors}_t + \phi(\text{Trips}_{t-1})
$$

## GAMs: Model Diagnostics

* As seen in the Autocorrelation Function (ACF) plots below, the GAM eliminates the severe serial correlation present in the traditional approach.

:::: {.columns}

::: {.column width="50%"}
![Traditional GLM (Severe Autocorrelation)](acf_good_plot.png){width=90%}
:::

::: {.column width="50%"}
![GAM (Corrected)](acf_bad_plot.png){width=90%}
:::

::::

## GAM Results: Drivers of Ridership

$$
\log(\mu_t) = \beta_0 + s(\text{Max Temp}_t) + \beta_1(\text{Light}_t) + \beta_2(\text{Precip}_t) + \boldsymbol{\gamma}(\text{Day}_t) + \phi(\text{Trips}_{t-1})
$$

<br>

| Parametric Predictor | Estimate | Std. Error | p-value |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Intercept | $3.598$ | $0.255$ | $<0.001$ |
| Usable Light (Hours) | $0.094$ | $0.018$ | $<0.001$ |
| Precipitation (Hours) | $-0.070$ | $0.008$ | $<0.001$ |
| Saturday | $0.265$ | $0.091$ | $0.004$ |
| Sunday | $0.322$ | $0.088$ | $<0.001$ |
| Lagged Trips ($t-1$) | $0.0004$ | $0.0001$ | $0.012$ |
| **Smooth Term** | **EDF** | **$\chi^2$** | **p-value** |
| Max Temperature ($^\circ$C) | $3.89$ | $540.5$ | $<0.001$ |

: Deviance Explained: 87.4% | Adj. $R^2 = 0.73$ | $n = 302$

## Seasonality of the Beach BIA

:::: {.columns}

::: {.column width="48%"}
![**Total Volume**](bike_season1.png)
:::

::: {.column width="48%"}
![**Velocity**](bike_season.png)
:::

::::

<br>

<small>
* **While the total volume plot confirms that summer is the busiest season, the velocity plot mathematically isolates dates where phases transition.**
</small>

## Seasonal Dates

**Isolating Seasonal Dates**
* The Negative Binomial GAM strips away daily weather fluctuations to reveal the true underlying ridership trend, incorporating a `day of year` variable to examine its direct effect.
* By using a restricted smooth spline ($k=5$), we force a clean curve to mathematically pinpoint the Summer Peak and the steep transitions of the Shoulder seasons.

| Phase | Date (2025) | Typical High | Temperature Range |
| :--- | :--- | :---: | :--- |
| Annual Minimum (Trough) | February 15 | $-3.6^\circ\text{C}$ | $-5.4^\circ\text{C}$ to $-2.0^\circ\text{C}$ |
| Peak Spring Growth | May 2 | $15.8^\circ\text{C}$ | $10.8^\circ\text{C}$ to $19.3^\circ\text{C}$ |
| Peak Summer Volume | July 15 | $29.6^\circ\text{C}$ | $28.7^\circ\text{C}$ to $32.0^\circ\text{C}$ |
| Autumn Deceleration | October 14 | $16.6^\circ\text{C}$ | $14.3^\circ\text{C}$ to $18.0^\circ\text{C}$ |

<br>
*(Roughly - On Season: May 2 to October 14, Shoulder Season: February 15 to May 2)*

## Bayesian GAM: Model Structure & Results

**Model Formulation**
$$
\log(\mu_t) = \beta_0 + s(\text{Max Temp}_t) + \beta_1(\text{Precip}_t) + \boldsymbol{\gamma}(\text{Day Category}_t) + \epsilon_t
$$
$$
\epsilon_t = \rho \epsilon_{t-1} + \nu_t \quad \text{(AR1 Residual Autocorrelation)}
$$

**Posterior Estimates (Poisson Family)**

| Predictor | Estimate | Error | 95% Credible Interval |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Intercept | $4.88$ | $0.04$ | $[4.81, 4.95]$ |
| Precipitation (Hours) | $-0.07$ | $0.01$ | $[-0.09, -0.06]$ |
| Saturday | $0.29$ | $0.07$ | $[0.16, 0.42]$ |
| Sunday | $0.43$ | $0.06$ | $[0.31, 0.56]$ |
| Statutory Holiday | $0.78$ | $0.13$ | $[0.53, 1.03]$ |
| AR(1) Autocorrelation ($\rho$) | $0.46$ | $0.07$ | $[0.33, 0.59]$ |

: Model converged successfully across 4 chains ($\hat{R} = 1.00$).

## Factors for Ridership: Weather & Calendar

:::: {.columns}

::: {.column width="48%"}
![Exponential growth as weather warms.](temp-bayes.png){width=60% fig-align="center"}
:::

::: {.column width="48%"}
![A 5-hour morning rain cuts volume by nearly half.](rain_bayes.png){width=60% fig-align="center"}
:::

::::

<br>

:::: {.columns}

::: {.column width="45%"}
![ ](ride_day_bayes.png){width=70% fig-align="center"}
:::

::: {.column width="50%"}
<small>

* **Weekday Baseline:** Consistent, lower-volume baseline.
* **Sunday Dominance:** Outperforms Saturdays, making it the highest-priority operational day.
* **Holiday Spikes:** Highest absolute volume, but widest credible interval (high variation based on weather).

</small>
:::

::::

## Insights for the Beach BIA

| Key Driver | Daily Impact | Business Application |
| :--- | :---: | :--- |
| **Statutory Holiday** | **<span style="color:#0000CC">+118%</span>** | <small>Particularly relevant for them to capture populations who visit the BIA, such as July 1st, for fireworks near Woodbine Beach.</small> |
| **Sunday Traffic** | **<span style="color:#0000CC">+53%</span>** | <small>The highest performing day. Target Sunday afternoons for major BIA activations.</small> |
| **Saturday Traffic** | **<span style="color:#0000CC">+33%</span>** | <small>A strong baseline weekend increase, but distinctly secondary to Sunday volume.</small> |
| **Hourly Rainfall** | **<span style="color:#CC0000">-6.8%</span>** | <small>BIA can tend to expect decreased traffic based on morning weather forecasts.</small> |

## Bayesian GAM Model: Casual vs. Annual Riders

:::: {.columns}

::: {.column width="48%"}
![**Temperature**<br><small>Casual ridership shows a delayed increase in trips compared to stable annual growth.</small>](user_type_temp.png)
:::

::: {.column width="48%"}
![**Precipitation**<br><small>Annual members maintain higher activity levels compared to casual riders.</small>](user_type_rain.png)
:::

::::

## Calendar Impact: Casual vs. Annual Riders

![ ](user_type_day.png){width=50% fig-align="center"}

* **Day:** Weekends and Holidays enable Casual riders, while Annual volume remains stable.
